full_text:
## **Applicant:** Andreas Hainari **Principal investigator:** Anniina Färkkilä

## Spatial biomarkers for stratified targeted therapy in ovarian cancer

**[Introduction]{.underline}**

High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains in need of new
targeted therapies, particularly for patients with homologous
recombination (HR)-proficient tumours who are ineligible for PARP
inhibitors (PARPi) or whose disease progresses despite them.
Antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) may provide an alternative, but their
clinical impact depends on more than just target abundance, including
tumour heterogeneity and the composition of the tumour microenvironment
(TME). Traditional bulk assays fail to capture the spatial distribution
of target-positive cells and their interactions with immune cells. To
overcome this limitation, we aim to map the in situ protein and
transcriptomic architecture using Tissue Cyclic Immunofluorescence
(t-CyCIF), bulk RNA sequencing, and GeoMx spatial transcriptomics to
understand the biological drivers of ADC target heterogeneity. By
defining how genetics and immune context shape the ADC therapy
landscape, we aim to provide a biological foundation for future patient
stratification.

**[Clinical rationale]{.underline}**

High relapse rates and limited durable treatment options characterise
HGSC. ADCs have emerged as a promising therapeutic class in HGSC, with

**[Clinical rationale]{.underline}**

High relapse rates and limited durable treatment options characterise
HGSC. ADCs have emerged as a promising therapeutic class in HGSC, with
significant clinical activity demonstrated through folate receptor α
(FRα)--targeted therapies, alongside emerging evidence for B7-H4-- and
TROP2--targeted approaches \[1, 2\].

However, ADC response is not fully predicted by bulk measures of target
expression alone. Clinical experience indicates that ADC efficacy is
dictated by tumour antigen expression heterogeneity, accessibility, and
TME factors, including stromal and immune components\[4\]. As a result,
patients with comparable bulk ADC target expression levels may exhibit
divergent treatment responses. These limitations suggest that current
biomarker-based bulk stratification strategies fail to capture the
biological determinants of ADC target distribution in HGSC.
Understanding how intra-tumoral heterogeneity, mutational landscapes,
and immune context govern ADC target accessibility is vital to optimise
patient stratification for ADC therapy.

**[Aims of the study]{.underline}**

This study aims to define spatial tumour and immune phenotypes
associated with ADC target expression and to integrate these phenotypes
with tumour genomic and transcriptional features to assess their
biological and clinical relevance.

**1.

associated with ADC target expression and to integrate these phenotypes
with tumour genomic and transcriptional features to assess their
biological and clinical relevance.

**1. Characterisation of spatial ADC expression phenotypes**

1.1. Quantify B7-H4, FRα, and TROP2 expression at single-cell resolution
using whole-slide t-CyCIF imaging and define reproducible spatial
expression phenotypes of the ADC targets along with extensive cell
phenotyping and functional state markers.

1.2. Characterise intra- and inter-tumour heterogeneity in ADC target
expression and spatial organisation across 109 samples using t-CyCIF
data.

1.3. Assess co-expression patterns among ADC targets using t-CyCIF,
GeoMx, and bulk RNA-sequencing data, and identify transcriptional
programmes associated with distinct ADC target expression phenotypes.

**2. Definition of spatial tumour--immune architecture and ADC
phenotypes**

2.1. Identify ADC target-positive tumour regions and quantify their
spatial organisation within the TME.

2.2. Characterise the immune context surrounding ADC target-positive
tumour cells, including immune infiltration, immune--tumour and
immune--immune spatial interactions, and cellular neighbourhood
organisation using the established spatial network--based SPACEstat
(Kang et al, in preparation) pipeline.

2.3. Define integrated spatial ADC phenotypes based on ADC target

rhood
organisation using the established spatial network--based SPACEstat
(Kang et al, in preparation) pipeline.

2.3. Define integrated spatial ADC phenotypes based on ADC target
expression, spatial organisation of target-positive tumour cells, and
their associated tumour--immune interactions.

**3. Genomic, transcriptional, and clinical integration**

3.1. Associate ADC phenotypes with tumour genomic features, including
copy-number--derived measures of genome instability and homologous
recombination status.

3.2. Validate ADC phenotypes using transcriptomics analyses derived from
bulk RNA-sequencing and GeoMx spatial transcriptomics samples.

3.3. Correlate ADC target spatial phenotypes to clinical variables,
including response to first-line platinum-based therapy and
overall/progression-free survival.

**[Materials and methods]{.underline}**

This study uses samples from the prospective Oncosys-OVA clinical study
(NCT06117384) at Helsinki University Hospital. Both fresh and
formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) samples from the primary and
interval debulking surgeries were collected. The study has been approved
by the Ethics Committee of Helsinki University Hospital (HUS/3334/2021).

**t-CyCIF multiplexed imaging (N=109) \[5\]** will be used to quantify
tumour, immune, and stromal cell phenotypes, as well as ADC-positive

i University Hospital (HUS/3334/2021).

**t-CyCIF multiplexed imaging (N=109) \[5\]** will be used to quantify
tumour, immune, and stromal cell phenotypes, as well as ADC-positive
tumour cells, at single-cell resolution across whole-slide images.
Images will be processed using TRIBUS \[6\] and analysed with SPACEstat
to derive spatial features describing ADC target expression phenotypes,
including the fraction and relative abundance of target-positive tumour
cells, spatial heterogeneity, tumour specificity, and spatial
organisation of ADC target expression. In parallel, spatial
tumour--immune architecture surrounding ADC target-positive regions will
be characterised through immune infiltration patterns, spatial proximity
analyses, cellular neighbourhood composition, and network topology to
define ADC-associated niche structures within the tumour
microenvironment.

**Bulk RNA-sequencing (N=301) and GeoMX spatial transcriptomics (53
samples; 498 tumour regions)** will be used to characterise intra-tumour
and inter-patient transcriptional heterogeneity in ADC target expression
and validate spatially defined ADC target phenotypes. These datasets
will additionally be used to assess co-expression across ADC targets and
identify transcriptional programmes associated with ADC expression
patterns. Direct integration of GeoMX and t-CyCIF data will enable the

sess co-expression across ADC targets and
identify transcriptional programmes associated with ADC expression
patterns. Direct integration of GeoMX and t-CyCIF data will enable the
linkage of spatially resolved protein expression with transcriptomic
profiles, providing insight into the molecular mechanisms underlying ADC
target heterogeneity and phenotypes.

**Whole-genome sequencing (N=301)** will be used to investigate genomic
associations with ADC target expression phenotypes and ADC
target-associated spatial niches, including homologous recombination
deficiency status, whole-genome doubling, fraction of genome altered,
and recurrent copy-number alterations. Genomic features will be
integrated with spatial phenotypes and RNA-sequence--defined groups to
identify molecular determinants of ADC target heterogeneity.

Finally, spatial, genomic, and transcriptional features will be
correlated with clinical variables, including treatment setting and
survival outcomes, using Cox proportional hazards models.

**[Preliminary results]{.underline}**

Preliminary tCyCIF analyses reveal pronounced spatial heterogeneity in
ADC target expression. Target expression is not uniform but is organised
into distinct spatial compartments, including multi-target--positive,
single-target--positive, and target-negative regions. These spatial

xpression is not uniform but is organised
into distinct spatial compartments, including multi-target--positive,
single-target--positive, and target-negative regions. These spatial
structures are not resolved in bulk data, indicating that bulk-level
expression masks underlying tissue-level organisation. The findings
suggest that ADC target expression is governed by spatially structured
tumour architecture rather than homogeneous cellular expression. In
addition, initial transcriptomics analyses support tumour-specific
expression of ADC targets but do not indicate widespread co-expression
of ADC targets on the bulk level (Spearman correlation analysis: ρ =
−0.16--0.21, p \> 0.1). These observations collectively highlight the
importance of spatial context in characterising ADC target
heterogeneity, prompting further investigation into the genomic
determinants (e.g., HR status) and immune interactions that shape these
distinct spatial
compartments.![](media/image1.png){width="2.905731627296588in"
height="1.7708333333333333in"}![](media/image2.png){width="1.947729658792651in"
